title: Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate
subtitle: The reported annual death rate from [infectious](#dod:communicable-diseases) and [parasitic diseases](#dod:parasitic-infection) per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates.
caption:
Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate, 1950 to 2023

The reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people, based on the underlying cause listed on death certificates.

Across seven countries between 1950 and 2023, the age-standardized annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people shows divergent trajectories, with one nation experiencing a massive temporary surge while others maintain low, declining levels. The vertical axis spans from 0 to 400 deaths per 100,000, capturing a wide range of mortality burdens. South Africa’s line begins around 1997 near 125, climbs steeply to a peak just under 400 around 2006, then falls rapidly to approximately 130 by 2023. In contrast, Brazil’s rate starts near 65 in 1980, declines gradually to about 30 by 2023, remaining consistently above all other countries except during South Africa’s spike. Russia’s line hovers between 20 and 30 from 1980 through 2023, showing minor fluctuations but no sustained rise or fall. The United States, France, Germany, and Australia all remain below 20 for the entire period shown; their lines cluster near the bottom of the chart, with values mostly under 10 after 1970. France and Germany begin near 40 in 1950, drop sharply to below 10 by 1970, and stay flat near 5 through 2023. Australia and the United States follow similar paths, starting near 40 in 1950, falling to under 10 by 1960, and remaining stable at low levels. The data excludes international aviation and notes that not all deaths may have been registered with a cause of death. The most visible pattern is the extreme deviation of South Africa’s mortality rate from the otherwise low and stable trends observed in the other six countries.
